coredata.dc:title: Enhanced catalytic activity of Fe3O4-carbon dots complex in the Fenton reaction for enhanced immunotherapeutic and oxygenation effects

coredata.dc:description: Tumor metastasis and recurrence are closely related to immune escape and hypoxia. Chemodynamic therapy (CDT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), and photothermal therapy (PTT) can induce immunogenic cell death (ICD), and their combination with immune checkpoint agents is a promising therapeutic strategy. Iron based nanomaterials have received more and more attention, but their low Fenton reaction efficiency has hindered their clinical application. In this study, Fe3O4-carbon dots complex (Fe3O4-CDs) was synthesized, which was modified with ferrocenedicarboxylic acid by amide bond, and crosslinked into Fe3O4-CDs@Fc nano complex. The CDs catalyzed the Fenton reaction activity of Fe3O4 by helping to improve the electron transfer efficiency, extended the reaction pH condition to 7.4. The Fe3O4-CDs@Fc exhibit exceptional optical activity, achieving a thermal conversion efficiency of 56.43 % under 808 nm light and a photosensitive single-line state oxygen quantum yield of 33 % under 660 nm light. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc improved intracellular oxygen level and inhibited hypoxia-inducing factor (HIF-1α) by in-situ oxygen production based on Fenton reaction. The multimodal combination of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (CDT/PDT/PTT) strongly induced immune cell death (ICD). The expression of immune-related protein and HIF-1α was investigated by immunofluorescence method. In vivo, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc combined with immune checkpoint blocker (antibody PD-L1, αPD-L1) effectively ablated primary tumors and inhibited distal tumor growth. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc is a promising immune-antitumor drug.

(c) Hysteresis maps comparing Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. (d) X-ray diffraction analysis of Fc, Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc, and standard card containing Fe3O4. (e) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy analysis of Fc, Fe3O4-CDs, and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. (f) Zeta potential measurements for Fc, Fe3O4, Fe3O4-CDs, and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. (g) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc.

(a) UV absorption spectra of Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. (d) Temperature change profiles of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc irradiated with different light sources (0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 W, 808 nm) for 0–10 min. (e) Temperature change of natural cooling of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc after 10 min of light exposure (1.0 W, 808 nm). (h) Photothermogram of dd H2O, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (concentration 200 µg mL−1) irradiated for 10 min at a light source (808 nm, 2 W). (i) Transmission electron microscopy of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc after acidolysis for 1 h in acidic solution. (j) Stability of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc in different solutions.

(a, b, c, d) Oxygen production capacity of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc examined by RDPP probe. (e) Fenton reaction mechanism of Fe2+. (f) Mechanism of the Fenton reaction catalyzed by Fe3O4-CDs. (g, h, i) Examination of •OH producing capacity of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc by MB probes. (j) ESR testing of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc for •OH production using DMPO as trapping agent. (k) DTNB was used as a probe to examine the ability of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc to consume glutathione at different pH values. (l) DTNB was used as a probe to examine the ability of different concentrations of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc to deplete glutathione. (p) Standard curve of degradation rate of DBPF in MB, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc and Fe3O4-CDs solutions, the degradation rate ln(At/A0) is linear with respect to the light time.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tumor metastasis and recurrence are closely related to immune escape and hypoxia. Chemodynamic therapy (CDT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), and photothermal therapy (PTT) can induce immunogenic cell death (ICD), and their combination with immune checkpoint agents is a promising therapeutic strategy. Iron based nanomaterials have received more and more attention, but their low Fenton reaction efficiency has hindered their clinical application. In this study, Fe3O4-carbon dots complex (Fe3O4-CDs) was synthesized, which was modified with ferrocenedicarboxylic acid by amide bond, and crosslinked into Fe3O4-CDs@Fc nano complex. The CDs catalyzed the Fenton reaction activity of Fe3O4 by helping to improve the electron transfer efficiency, extended the reaction pH condition to 7.4. The Fe3O4-CDs@Fc exhibit exceptional optical activity, achieving a thermal conversion efficiency of 56.43 % under 808 nm light and a photosensitive single-line state oxygen quantum yield of 33 % under 660 nm light. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc improved intracellular oxygen level and inhibited hypoxia-inducing factor (HIF-1α) by in-situ oxygen production based on Fenton reaction. The multimodal combination of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (CDT/PDT/PTT) strongly induced immune cell death (ICD). The expression of immune-related protein and HIF-1α was investigated by immunofluorescence method. In vivo, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc combined with immune checkpoint blocker (antibody PD-L1, αPD-L1) effectively ablated primary tumors and inhibited distal tumor growth. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc is a promising immune-antitumor drug.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tumor invasion and metastasis are closely related to tumor immune escape and hypoxia [1–3]. Immune cells play a crucial role in recognizing and eliminating tumors to inhibit their invasion and distal spread [4]. Although immunotherapy has brought hope for cancer treatment, it is unable to benefit because most patients lack an immune response [5]. Therefore, activating the host immune system becomes the key to achieving successful treatment outcomes. It has been reported in the literature that chemodynamic therapy (CDT) [6–9], photodyna-mic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) can induce immunogenic cell death (ICD) by damaging tumor cells, thereby triggering an antigenic response and activating the host immune system. ICD inducers combined with immune adjuvants have become a promising clinical treatment strategy [10–14]. Iron-based nanomaterials have become widely used in biomedical fields due to their unique physical and chemical properties and excellent biocompatibility [15]. Iron-based nanomaterials can decompose hydrogen peroxide through the Fenton reaction, producing cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (•OH) and oxygen, causing cell damage, inducing immunogenic cell death (ICD), and alleviating tumor hypoxia. However, Fenton reaction occurs under acidic conditions, and the optimal pH range is required to be 2–4 [16]. However, the acidity of tumor microenvironment (TME) is low, and the pH value is between 5.5 and 6.8. Therefore, the Fenton reaction efficiency of iron-based nanomaterials is low in tumor, which seriously reduces the effect of CDT and in-situ oxygenation [17]. Therefore, how to improve the Fenton reaction efficiency of iron-based nanomaterials in TME is the key problem. In this study, in order to improve the Fenton reaction efficiency in TME and relieve tumor hypoxia, a one-pot process was used to prepare Fe3O4-carbon dots @ferrocene nano complex (Fe3O4-CDs@Fc). Under the carbon dots (CDs) catalysis, the Fenton-reaction efficiency was significantly improved, and the pH condition required for the reaction was expanded from acidic to alkalescence (pH 7.4). The Fe3O4-CDs@Fc nano complex enhanced Fenton reaction effect and alleviated tumor hypoxia. Under the influence of the external magnetic field, these nanocomplex are passively targeted and enriched at the tumor site [18,19]. In addition, the prepared CDs have PDT and PTT functions, so the CDT/PDT/PTT synergistic effect significantly enhances ICD. Carbon dots, as novel carbon nanomaterials with sizes smaller than 10 nm, exhibit excellent photoluminescence properties, remarkable biocompatibility, and abundant surface functional groups. These characteristics make them highly promising in the fields of nano-loading systems and biofluorescence imaging [20,21]. Previous studies have reported that metal-doped carbon dots possess an abundance of π-electrons and free electrons from metals on their surfaces, which can accelerate electron transfer and catalyse redox reactions [22]. Ferrocene is an excellent reagent for Fenton reaction. The 3d orbital of iron ion overlapped with the π-delocalized orbital of ligand, so iron ion is bounded by ligand and could not form free iron ion, thus preventing the loss of iron due to precipitation as Fe (OH)x during the circulation in the body [23]. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc can effectively ablated primary tumors, increased oxygen in situ, inhibited the expression of HIF-1α, and effectively induced ICD. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc was combine with the immune checkpoint blocker α-PD-L1 to effectively inhibit distal tumor growth (Scheme 1 ).

All chemicals from commercial sources were of reagent grade and used without further purification. Ethylenediamine, reduced glutathione (GSH), NaOH, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (RDPP) were all purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). 1,1′-ferrocenedicarboxylic acid was purchased from shyuanye (Shanghai, China).N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and ethanol were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). H2O2 (30 %) was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., ltd. (Shenzhen, China). 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DT NB) and 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). The GSH assay kit was purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. Calcein-AM, PI, Trizol, Calreticulin (CRT) antibody, CCK-8 kit, β-Actin antibody and Hematoxylin/Eosin Staining Kit (H&E) were all purchased from Beyotime(Shanghai, China). Ammonium ferric citrate, 2,7-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and penicillinum-streptomycin were bought in Yu Bo Biotecnology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). The newborn bovine serum, PBS, RPMI-1640 media and trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology (Beijing, China). Anti-HIF-1α-AF488 conjugated antibody was acquired through Bioss (Beijing, China). Dialysis Membranes (500 Da) was provided by Biotopped Life Sciences. Interleukin-2(IL-2), CD8, Arginase-1(ARG-1) antibody are products of ImmunoWay Biotechnology Company.

UV-2600 Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) are used to characterize optical properties. Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) are utilized for the examination of characteristic functional groups. A small cryogenic high-speed centrifuge from Eppendorf in Germany is used along with the Leica DM IL IED fluorescence inverted microscope to observe cell imaging. For histological research purposes, instruments such as the Leica HistoCore PEARL automatic tissue dehydrator, Leica HistoCore Arcadia H paraffin embedding machine, Leica HistoCore AUTOCUT R automatic rotating microtome, and Leica DM4 Borthomosaic microscope are employed. All instruments from the Leica series are supplied by Leica Microsystems Trading Co., located in Shanghai, China. AniView100 multimodal animal in vivo imaging system used for in vivo fluorescence in Mice.

Ferric ammonium citrate (0.262 mg) and 800 μL of ethylenediamine were added to a 50 mL flask. Then, 20 mL of deionized water was added and dispersed by ultrasonication for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL high pressure reactor and heated to 200 °C for a duration of 14 h. After the reaction, the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. The collected sediments were washed twice with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, respectively. Subsequently, it was dialyzed in water used a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 72 h to remove any unreacted compounds before being freeze-dried to obtain Fe3O4-CDs.

Fe3O4-CDs (30 mg) were added into a 100 mL round-bottomed flask, followed by the addition of 80 mL of 1,4-dioxane. The mixture was then ultrasonically dispersed for 20 min. Subsequently, 0.1 g of 1,1′-ferrocenedicarbonyl dichloride and another 20 mL of 1,4-dioxane were added and ultrasonically dispersed for an additional duration of 30 min. Next, another portion consisting of 0.1 g of 1,1′-ferrocenedicarbonyl chloride and an additional volume of 20 mL of the solvent was introduced into the system. This solution was dropwise added to the Fe3O4-CDs under anaerobic conditions while stirring at a temperature maintained at approximately 35 °C in an oil bath for a period lasting up to 24 h. Afterward, it was naturally cooled down to room temperature before being dialyzed in a dialysis bag (500 Da) containing a solution composed of 0.01 M NaHCO3 for 48 h. Following this step, it underwent further dialysis used deionized water over a span of 48 h. The final product was obtained through freeze-drying and is referred to as Fe3O4-CDs@Fc.

RDPP was used as a probe to detect the oxygen-producing capacity of the materials, and the samples to be tested Free Fe3+ (FeCl3), free Fc, free Fe3O4, Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc were prepared with deionized water at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 of the samples to be tested. 100 µL of the sample to be tested and 50 µL of RDPP ethanol solution (10 × 10−3 M) were added to a 3 mL cuvette, diluted to 3 mL with PBS buffer solution at different pH (5.5, 6.8, 7.4), the cuvette was closed with a stream of nitrogen to exclude the oxygen, and 50 mmol of H2O2 was added under air-isolation, and the cuvettes were rested for 30 min, and then immediately measured using a The intensity of red fluorescence (615 nm) was detected by fluorescence spectrophotometer immediately after 30 min of resting (the excitation light source was green light λex = 455 nm), and the intensity was recorded every 3 min. Fluorescence imaging was taken with a digital camera by irradiating the samples with green light. In addition, 100 µL of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc with 50 µL of RDPP was added to a 3 mL cuvette, diluted to 3 mL with pH = 5.5 PBS, and the samples were exposed to 808 nm laser light for 15 min (light power 1 W), continued to be stationary for 15 min, and then the fluorescence intensity was recorded immediately by fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hydroxyl radical production by MB
ce:section.ce:para: MB was used as the ·OH trapping agent. The substances to be measured are free Fe3O4, free Fc, Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc, all of which are made into three concentration solutions (50, 100,150 and 200 µg mL−1). 200 µL of the solution to be tested, 200 µL of MB (10 µg mL−1), 200 µL of H2O2 (100 mM) were added to the cuvette, diluted to 3 mL with PBS (pH = 5.5, 6.8, 7.4) and left to stand at room temperature for 30 min, and the absorbance at 650 nm was recorded by UV–visible spectrophotometer. In addition, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc samples (300 µg mL−1, pH = 5.5) were exposed to laser light for 10 min (808 nm, power 1 W), and other experimental procedures were unchanged.

The ability of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc to produce •OH was determined by ESR, and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as a trapping agent for hydroxyl radicals·H2O2 (100 mM), Fe3O4-CDs (1 mg mL−1), Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (1 mg mL−1), Fe3O4 (1 mg mL−1), and Fc (1 mg mL−1) were formulated separately using water as solvent. 200 μL of material was taken, 200 μL of H2O2 was added, and the volume was fixed to 3.98 mL with PBS of different pH, and after sufficient reaction, 10 μL of DMPO was added, and the ESR assay was performed immediately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH depletion capacity by DTNB
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc to deplete GSH was assayed using DTNB as a probe. 140 µL of aqueous GSH solution (20 µM), 100 µL of the sample to be tested (concentration gradient of 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg mL−1) were taken and then diluted to 3 mL with PBS solution at pH = 5.5, mixed and reacted at room temperature for 30 min, then 60 µL of DTNB (20 µM) was added to sufficiently react the reaction for 10 min, and UV–visible spectroscopic photometer to record the absorbance at 325 nm and 412 nm. 140 µL of GSH aqueous solution (20 µM), 100 µL of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (200 μg mL−1) were taken and then diluted to 3 mL with PBS of different pH (5.5, 6.8, 7.4), and mixed and reacted for 30 min at room temperature, and then 60 µL of DTNB (20 µM) was added to completely react for 10 min, and the absorbance was recorded by UV visible spectrophotometer to record the absorbance at 325 nm and 412 nm.

CT26 cells (mouse colon cancer cell line ATCC CRL-2638) and L929 cells (mouse fibroblast cell line ATCC CCL-1) were provided by Harbin Institute of Technology. CT26 cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10 % FBS and 1 % double antibody (penicillin–streptomycin) in an incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. The cells were digested and reapplied to the wall every 24 h. Experiments were performed when the cell density reached 80–90 %.

The cells were divided into two groups: dark group and light group. The original culture medium was aspirated, and 1 mL of dispersions of different materials (Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc) with different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200 μg mL−1) were added to the cell culture flasks containing cells in logarithmic growth phase, and incubated for 24 h for the dark group, and 4 h for the light group, and then irradiated for 10 min with light (808 nm, optical power density 50 mW cm−2). After 4 h of incubation, the light group was irradiated with a light source (808 nm, optical power density 50 mW cm−2) for 10 min. The incubation was continued for 20 h. The medium containing the materials and the cell suspension made from the digested cells in the corresponding flasks were collected, respectively, and both were mixed, and the supernatant was centrifuged at 1000 rpm min−1 for 10 min, the cell precipitate was retained, and the cell precipitate was washed twice with PBS and collected by centrifugation, and the cells were suspended by adding 0.5 mL of 0.1 M isotonic PBS buffer, and the cell suspension was The cells were suspended in 0.5 mL of 0.1 M isotonic PBS buffer, and the cell suspension was broken by ultrasonication in an ice-water bath: power 300 W, 3–5 s time−1, 30 s intervals, and repeated 3–5 times; the broken homogenate was taken, and the GSH content in the cells was determined according to the instructions of the GSH content assay kit.

Cells in the logarithmic growth phase were inoculated in six-well plates (1 × 106 mL−1) and incubated at 37 °C in an incubator with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The medium was discarded, and 1 mL of different nanomaterials (Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc) at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, and 200 µg mL−1) were added and incubated for 4 h in an incubator with 5 % CO2. CO2 incubator for 4 h. Then the light group was incubated for 4 h with laser irradiation for 10 min (808 nm, optical power density 50 mW cm−2; 660 nm, optical power density 50 mW cm−2), washed 3 times with PBS, and 1 mL of reactive oxygen probe DCFH-DA (50 µg mL−1) was added to each well to continue incubation for 30 min, and then observed and photographed under the fluorescence inverted microscope. ​And photographed under fluorescence inverted microscope. The intracellular reactive oxygen species were examined in the absence of light in a similar manner.

Cells incubated in six-well plates were aspirated to discard the original medium, and 1 mL of different nanomaterials (Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc) of the same concentration (200 µg mL−1) were added respectively, and incubated for 4 h. The cells were divided into light-free and light-exposed groups, and the light-exposed group was illuminated with 808 nm laser (light intensity optical power density 50 mW cm−2) for 10 min. The cells were divided into light-free and light-illuminated groups, and the light-illuminated group was illuminated with 808 nm laser (Optical power density 50 mW cm−2) for 10 min, and continued to be cultured for 4 h. The light-free group was continued to be cultured during this process. The supernatant was discarded, 500 µL of propionic acid iodide (PI) at a concentration of 4.5 µM and 500 µL of calcein acetoxymethyl ester (AM) at a concentration of 2 µM were added, mixed and incubated for 10 min, and the cells were washed with PBS for three times, and then photographed for observation under the fluorescence inverted microscope.

CT26 cells in logarithmic growth phase were inoculated in 6-well plates (1 × 106 mL−1) and incubated in an anaerobic incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The medium was discarded and washed with PBS, and the cells were divided into two groups: the dark group, and the light group (808 nm, optical power density 50 mW cm−2, light irradiation for 10 min). Added 1 mL of Fe3O4-CDs or Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (0, 50, 100 and 200 µg mL−1), respectively, and continue to incubate for 4 h. Wash the cells with PBS for 3 times, and then stain them with 50 µL of RDPP (10 µg mL−1) for 10 min, and then wash them with PBS, and then observe them and take pictures under a fluorescence inverted microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunofluorescence assay of CRT expression on the surface of CT26 cells
ce:section.ce:para: CT26 cells in logarithmic growth phase were inoculated in 6-well plates (1 × 106 mL−1) and incubated in an incubator at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The medium was aspirated and washed with PBS, and the cells were divided into two groups: the dark group and the light group. Different concentrations of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (0, 50, 100 and 200 µg mL−1) were added, respectively, and the dark group was incubated in an incubator at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The light group was incubated for 4 h and then irradiated with laser light for 10 min (808 nm, 50 mW cm−2; 660 nm, optical power density 50 mW cm−2), and the cells were incubated for 20 h. After aspirating and discarding the drugs, the cells were washed with PBS for 3 times, AF 488-NHS Ester was added, and the cells were stained for 20 min, and the cells were washed with PBS for 3 times, and 2.5 % glutaraldehyde was added to fix the cells for 10 min. The cells were washed with PBS after aspirating the glutaraldehyde, and the cells were stained by adding DAPI (2 µg mL−1) for 10 min, and then observed and photographed under the fluorescence microscope.

Stimulation of apoptosis induces the release of intracellular ATP from tumor cells into the extracellular compartment, and ATP promotes phagocytosis of apoptotic cells, thereby triggering specific anti-tumor immunocidal effects. The immunotherapy effect was assessed by ATP release. CT26 cells in logarithmic growth phase were inoculated in 6-well plates (1 × 106 mL−1) and incubated in an incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The medium was aspirated and washed with PBS, and the cells were divided into two groups: dark group and light group. 1 mL of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (50, 100, 200 µg mL−1, 200 µg mL−1 + Laser) were added respectively, and the dark group was incubated for 24 h in an incubator at 37 °C and 5 % CO2, while the light group was incubated for 4 h and then irradiated by laser light for 10 min (808 nm, optical power density 50 mW cm−2; 660 nm, optical power density 50 mW cm−2) and co-incubated for 20 h. After 4 h of irradiation, ATP release from CT26 cells was measured according to the procedure of the ATP assay kit.

HIF-1α overexpression is a typical feature of TME-induced hypoxia in cancer cells. The degree of HIF-1α expression can be used to assess the intracellular hypoxic environment. Cells in logarithmic growth phase were inoculated in six-well plates (1 × 106 mL−1) and incubated in an incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. CT26 cells were co-cultured with 1 mL (0, 50, 75, 100, 200 μg mL−1, 200 μg mL−1 + Laser) of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc for 4 h(optical power density 50mW cm−2). The culture solution was aspirated, and the culture solution was washed with PBS for washing. Then, 100 μL of anti-HIF-1α/AF488 was added to stain the cells for 40 min. 2.5 % glutaraldehyde was added to fix the cells for 10 min, after which 1 mL of DAPI (2 μg mL−1) was added to stain the cells for 10 min. The culture medium was discarded and washed three times with PBS, and finally, the cells were observed by fluorescence inverted microscope and photographed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging and photothermal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Female Balb/c mice (6–7 weeks old, 18–20 g) were selected as experimental subjects, and CT26 cells were chosen as the in vitro cell line. When the cells were in the logarithmic growth phase, the cells were digested, suspended in PBS (about 1 × 107 mL−1), and subcutaneously injected 100 μL (about 1 million cells) into the right buttock of the mice, respectively. When the tumor volume reached 100 mm3, the drug (Fe3O4-CDs@Fc) was administered via the tail vein at a dose of 100 µL (100 µg mL−1), and after anaesthetising the mice with ether, the tumor site of the mice was exposed to a magnetic field with magnets, and the distribution of the drug in the body was observed by in vivo bioluminescence imaging. The mice were exposed to Laser for 10 min (laser power 1 W, optical power density 50 mW cm−2), and the temperature of the tumor was recorded by thermal imager and the thermal image was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of animal double tumor model
ce:section.ce:para: Female Balb/c mice (6–7 weeks old, 18–20 g) were selected, and CT26 cells were chosen as the in vitro cell line. When the cells were in the logarithmic growth phase, the cells were digested down, suspended in PBS (about 1 × 107 mL−1), and subcutaneously injected with 100 μL (about 1 million cells) into the two sides of the rump of the mice, respectively. The right side was defined as the treatment group (primary tumor) and the left side as the no treatment group (distal tumor). When the tumor volume reached 100 mm3, the mice were randomly divided into 6 groups (5 mice in each group) according to the experiment: the PBS group, the Fe3O4-CDs, the Fe3O4-CDs@Fc group, the αPD-L1 group and the αPD-L1 + Fe3O4-CDs@Fc group, and the αPD-L1 + Fe3O4-CDs@Fc + 808 nm + 660 nm laser group (power density 50 mW cm−2). The right tumor was administered in situ with all drugs at a concentration of 100 μg mL−1, and the administered dose was 100 μL per mouse. The drugs were administered in situ on days 1 and 7, and αPD-L1 was injected via the abdomen at a dose of 75 mg per mouse 4 h after the administration. The volume of the tumor and the body weight of the mice were recorded daily, and the mice's status was observed. Mice were sacrificed and the hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, and kidneys were removed after 14 days, proximal tumors, and distal tumors were photographed and imaged and weighed.

After 14 days of treatment, the mice were put to death and the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs and kidneys) and tumors were removed from each group of mice and the tumors were weighed and photographed. Intact tumor tissues were analysed by immunofluorescence staining. Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining was used to analyse the potential toxicity of the nanomedicine to tumors and major organs.

CD8 expression assay, IL-2 expression assay, ARG-1 expression assay, CRT expression assay, and HIF-1α expression assay: mice were put to death after 14 days of treatment, and the tumor tissues of the different treatment groups were made into paraffin sections, and then subjected to deprivation, antigen repair, fixation, and sealing in sequence; after incubation of the tumor tissues with primary and secondary antibodies according to the instructions of the kit, the tumor tissues were subjected to immunofluorescent staining and analysed by using fluorescence inverted microscope.

The preparation of multifunctional nanoplatforms is generally challenging. The assembly process of different functional units by physical adsorption or chemical bonding is very complicated, and the loading rate is limited by various factors [24]. In this study complex nanoparticles (Fe3O4-CDs) were conveniently obtained using a one-pot method as illustrated in Scheme 1. In the reactor, ammonium ferric citrate and ethylenediamine form CDs through a complex process of coupling-polymerization-crosslinking-carbonization, while iron ions undergo a process of hydrolysis-dehydration to form Fe3O4. Fe3O4 and CDs symbiosis to form heterojunction, Fe3O4-CDs. Ferrocene dicarboxylic acid (Fc) was introduced to the surface of Fe3O4-CDs by amide bonds and ester bonds to form Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (Scheme 1). Fig. 1 a is a transmission electron microscope (TEM) of Fe3O4-CDs, Fig. 1a illustrates that Fe3O4-CDs are uniformly elliptical particles with a particle size ranging from 3 to 6 nm. TEM analysis in Fig. 1b reveals that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc possess uniform spherical morphology with a particle size distribution within the range of 3.5–7 nm. Notably, there is minimal change observed in particle size after Fc molecule modification. Fig. 1a and 1b are high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The images show that the lattice spacing of Fe3O4 is d = 0.245 nm, corresponding to the (311) crystal plane, and the lattice spacing of CDs is d = 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphene (100) crystal plane. Fe3O4 nanoparticles are closely bound to CDs. It was confirmed that Fe3O4 nanoparticles successfully combined with CDs [25,26]. The hysteresis return line measured with a vibrating sample magnetometer is shown in Fig. 1c, indicating that the magnetization curves of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc are completely overlapped, all passing through the centre point without any coercivity or remanent magnetism. This observation confirms the superparamagnetic nature of these materials. Additionally, the Fe3O4-CDs@Fc were uniformly dispersed in methanol and exhibited adhesion to the wall under an external magnetic field (inset in Fig. 1c), further demonstrating their superparamagnetism. In Fig. 1d, the XRD pattern reveals a broad blunt peak at 2θ = 24.70° corresponding to the graphite crystal (002) facet [25], while diffraction peaks at 2θ = 29.90°, 35.28°, 42.88°, 56.69°, and 62.25° match those of the magnetite crystal structure (PDF#19-0629) precisely [25], it was confirmed that CDs and Fe3O4 crystals were successfully combined. The Raman spectrum of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc was shown in Fig. S1b. The D-peak and G-peak of graphene were located at 1350 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1, respectively, and the intensity of G-peak was greater than that of D-peak, indicated successful carbonization and a higher degree of graphitization. Additionally, the characteristic peaks at 207 cm−1 and 270 cm−1 correspond to Fe3O4, further confirming the successful one-step hydrothermal preparation of Fe3O4-CDs. Fig. 1e is an infrared spectrum shown Fe-O tensile vibration at 587 cm−1 in Fe3O4-CDs. The characteristic peaks of Fe3O4-CDs were enhanced by Fc modification: carboxyl group at 1673 cm−1 and CH of CH2 at 2750–2860 cm−1. The OH and NH vibration peak of Fe3O4-CDs at 3200–3600 cm−1 becomes COOH and NH2. The CO vibration peak at 1673 cm−1 is also enhanced when Fc is modified, indicating the presence of COOH group on the surface of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. The peak shape at 3200–3600 cm−1 was asymmetrical, suggested the possible presence of NH2, and we found an electron diffraction peak (403.3 eV) of NH in High-resolution XPS of N (Fig. S2b), indicated the presence of an amino group. These findings support the successful surface modification of Fe3O4-CDs by Fc. Furthermore, Fig. 1f illustrates that upon Fc modification on the surface of Fe3O4-CDs, there is an exponential increase in zeta potentials indicating a significant enhancement in positive charge due to ferrocene’s positively charged metal ion centre. Fig. 1g illustrates the XPS analysis of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc, where peaks at 285.39 eV, 399.39 eV, 531.39 eV, 711.39 eV, and 726.39 eV correspond to C 1 s, N 1p, O 1 s, Fe 2p3/2, and Fe 2p1/2 respectively. This clearly indicates that the composition of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc includes elements such as C, N, O, and Fe in percentages of approximately: C (46.56 %), N (4.65 %), O (33.05 %), and Fe (15.74 %). Add-.

itionally in Fig. S2a–d we present HRXPS spectra for C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Fe 2p, respectively. In Fig. S2d, the peaks at 731.09 eV and 714.89 eV correspond to Fe2+ 2p1/2 and Fe2+ 2p3/2, respectively. The peaks at 724.99 eV and 710.99 eV are attributed to Fe3+2p1/2 and Fe3+2p3/2, respectively. Notably, in Fig. S2d, the satellite peak at 741.09 eV is direct proof for the existence of Fe3+. The thermogravimetric analysis curve in Fig. S3a reveals a subtle inflection at 200 °C, indicated the evaporation of surface water from the sample with a weight loss of approximately 4.9 %. At 300-600℃, the weight of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc was reduced by 33.8 %, which is due to surface organic functional groups or polymers. The mass is constant between 600 and 800 °C, and this mass is about 61.3 %, which is attributed to Fe3O4. The loading rate of Fc is about 25.16 % by using the standard curve of concentration-absorbance of Fc (Fig. S3b).

The optical properties of carbon points are excellent because they exhibit absorption in both the ultraviolet and visible regions, as well as superior emission characteristics. When in the excited state, carbon points emit heat or light through non-radiative or radiative transitions, showing great potential in the field of photothermal conversion and luminous materials [27,28]. The absorption spectra of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc are shown in Fig. 2 a. The presence of π-π* absorption at 295 nm indicates the formation of SP2-hybridized carbon core, the n-π* absorption at 395 nm suggests the presence of functional groups on the surface containing heteroatoms such as N, O, and other heteroatoms. The absorption peaks of sp2 conjugate domains extended to the near-infrared region (400–850 nm). The redshift and widening of absorption peaks can be attributed to surface defects and electron pulling functional group effects. The combination of Fe3O4 and CDs creates a semiconductor heterostructure with a narrower band gap [29,30], allowing for more efficient electron transfer to the conduction band under light irradiation. This results in increased photon absorption efficiency and absorbance, while the electrical conductivity of carbon dots enhances electron-hole separation and suppresses fluorescence emission, ultimately leading to improved photothermal conversion efficiency [31]. The fluorescence emission spectrum is depicted in Fig. 2b, and the emission wavelength exhibits a significant red-shift as the excitation wavelength increases, demonstrating clear dependence on the excitation wavelength. Moreover, there is a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. When excited by green light (490–510 nm), the dominant peaks of emitted light are observed in the green and red regions (550–720 nm). Therefore, green light was selected for excitation during subsequent cell phagocytosis experiments under a fluorescence microscope. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc has remark-able photothermal properties, as evidenced by the concen-tration-dependent behaviour shown in Fig. 2c and the light-source dependent photothermal conversion capability shown in Fig. 2d. After 10 min of laser irradiation (λ = 808 nm, power 2 W), the control group (H2O) showed negligible change in temperature, while the Fe3O4-CDs@Fc aqueous solution (300 µg mL−1) exhibited a significant increase in temperature from room temperature to 63 °C, demonstrated a robust photothermal conversion capability. Fig. 2e illustrates the photothermal conversion results of the Fe3O4-CDs@Fc solution (200 µg mL−1) under irradiation for 10 min at λ = 808 nm and power of 1.5 W. The sample’s temperature rose to 57 °C during illumination and took approximately 23 min to naturally cool down to room temperature. The sample was recycled for five times within a total duration of 165 min (Fig. 2f), with consistent highest and lowest temperatures observed in each cycle. The maximum and minimum temperatures of each cycle were consistently similar, indicating the stable photothermal conversion efficiency of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. Fig. 2g illustrates the standard curve of photothermal conversion with a linear regression equation of y = 299.700kx + 65.709 and a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.991. The calculated photothermal conversion efficiency η = 56.43 % for Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (see Supplementary information for calculation details). The photothermal imaging plots of the control (H2O) and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (200 µg mL−1) are shown in Fig. 2h. While the temperature change in the control group was negligible, the experimental group exhibited a significant increase in temperature from room temperature to above 60 °C after 10 min of light exposure, further demonstrating its excellent photothermal conversion ability. In Fig. 2i, high magnification transmission electron micrographs of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc in acidic solution (pH = 5.5) are presented. Under acidic conditions, edge diffusion of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc resulted in an increase in particle size while maintaining their particle state. This indicates that in this state, the exposed Fe3O4 decomposes and causes edge diffusion, leading to the increase of nanoparticle size. However, the internal Fe3O4-CDs heterojunction maintains the spherical shape of the nanoparticles through surface-modified cross-linked Fc, thus preventing the loss of iron sources. Fig. 2j illustrated the dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc in various solutions, revealing an increase in particle size under weakly acidic conditions (pH = 5.5), consistent with Fig. 2i. However, the particle size remains largely unchanged in water, NaCl solution, and cell culture medium, indicating stability in these environments. Additionally, Fig. S5b, c demonstrates that after 7 days at room temperature and under natural light exposure, there are no significant alterations observed in absorption spectra, zeta potential values or coloration for Fe3O4-CDs@Fc across different solutions-highlighting their excellent stability and resistance to photobleaching.

Fe3O4-CDs@Fc catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 to produce O2 and •OH by Fenton reaction. Therefore, an evaluation was conducted on both O2 producing capacity and •OH producing capacity to analyse the influence of CDs on the Fenton reaction. Dynamic fluorescence probe RDPP was used to evaluate the oxygen production capacity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc [32]. Photoluminescence emits red fluorescence in the absence of oxygen, and fluorescence quenching in the presence of oxygen. The intensity of red fluorescence is inversely proportional to the oxygen content.

The intervention of pH and photothermal effect on Fe3O4-CDs@Fc Fenton reaction was investigated and compared with free Fe3+, free Fe3O4, free Fc and Fe3O4-CDs. All experimental samples were treated anaerobic. Fig. 3 a shown that in the solution with pH = 7.4, the fluorescence intensity of free Fe3+, free Fe3O4 and free Fc remains unchanged, indicated that Fenton reaction cannot occur under the condition of pH = 7.4 (physiological condition). In a slightly acidic solution with pH = 6.8 (at this experimental dose), a weaker Fenton reaction occurred. In a solution with a pH = 5.5, the Fenton reaction was enhanced, but the activity was still weak. The activity of Fc is slightly higher than free Fe3+ and Fe3O4. It can be seen that their Fenton-reaction occurs under acidic conditions, and the reaction activity is positively correlated with acidity. Surprisingly, Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc showed Fenton activity even in solutions with pH = 7.4. The reactivity was stronger when the acidity increased from pH 7.4 to 5.5. As shown in Fig. 3b, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc solution temperature rises from room temperature to 57 °C under laser irradiation for 7 min (λ = 808 nm, power 1.5 W). At the same time, Fenton reaction activity increases by about 100 %, indicated that photothermal effect promoted Fenton reaction. The pH-dependent oxygen production capacity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc was illustrated in Fig. 3c, indicating that Fenton activity increases with the increase of acidity. The RDPP fluorescence imaging results of different samples are shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence gradually decreases in the order of Fe3O4, Fc, Fe3O4-CDs, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. In contrast, Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc showed more obvious fluorescence quenching, and the latter was almost completely quenched. The above results show that the Fenton activity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc and Fe3O4-CDs was greater than that of Free Fe3+, Fe3O4 and Fc, which was obviously the result of CDs catalysis. However, the specific catalytic mechanism remains unclear. We speculate that CDs contributes to improved electron transport efficiency (Fig. 3e and 3f) [33,34]. The CDs is a sp2 hybrid conjugated system, and π electrons move freely in the conjugated domains of carbon nuclei. FeO and Fe2O3 coexist in Fe3O4-CDs, and FeO forms a covalent bond with CDs to make up for the oxygen vacancy in Fe3O4. The presence of oxygen vacancies in FeO leads to the formation of freely moving electrons in the d orbital. Thus, Fe3O4-CDs exhibited the characteristics of a multi-electron system, which facilitated efficient electron transfer between hydrogen H2O2 and Fe ions, thus catalyzed the Fenton reaction (Fig. 3 e and Fig. 3f). In addition, the photogenerated electrons of CDs enter the d orbitals of Fe3O4, which improved the photogenerated carrier separation efficiency, and thus improved the photothermal conversion efficiency.

Subsequently, the capacity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc to generate •OH was examined using methylene blue (MB) [35]. MB is a •OH trapping agent with significant absorption at 650 nm, and the absorption peak disappears after capturing •OH. Therefore, the intensity of this absorption peak is negatively correlated with the amount of •OH. As shown in Fig. 3g and 3i, with the increased of the dose Fe3O4-CDs@Fc and acidity of solution, the concentration of •OH significantly increased, indicated that the ability to generate •OH is positively correlated with the dose and acidity. Fig. 3h shown that photothermal effect increases the degradation rate of MB and promotes Fenton reaction. It is worth noting that free Fe3O4 exhibits limited •OH producing capability, whereas Fe3O4-CDs significantly enhances this capacity. These findings clearly indicate that carbon dots enhance the Fenton reaction efficiency of Fe3O4, which aligns with the outcomes obtained from RDPP experiments (Fig. 3a–d). To investigate the ability of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc to generate •OH under varying pH conditions, ESR analysis was conducted [36]. The hydroxyl radical trapping agent, DMPO, was employed and the ESR spectrum exhibited four peaks in a 1:2:2:1 ratio as shown in Fig. 3j. Notably, the Fe3O4-CDs@Fc displayed the strongest signal, indicating enhanced reactivity with increasing acidity. This observation is consistent with the findings from the MB experiment, RDPP experiment and fluorescence imaging experiment (Fig. 3a–d, Fig. 3g–i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The depletion of GSH by Fe3O4-CDs@Fc
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant GSH is present in high concentrations in tumor cells. GSH plays a crucial role in maintaining intracellular redox balance and inhibiting oxidative stress. GSH is particularly sensitive to exogenous reactive oxygen species (ROS), which is a major challenge for therapies such as CDT and PDT [23]. Excess metal ions with varying valence states and organic matter containing SS bonds serve as essential reagents for depleting GSH levels [37,38]. DTNB, a commonly used GSH probe [39], can be reduced to NTB by GSH. DTNB exhibits UV absorption at 312 nm while NTB absorbs light at 425 nm. The absorbance of DTNB is negatively correlated with GSH concentration, while the absorbance of DTNB is positively correlated with the GSH consumption capacity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. The capacity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc to deplete GSH was evaluated under various conditions using DTNB. As depicted in Fig. 3k and Fig. 3l, the depletion of GSH by Fe3O4-CDs@Fc was dose-dependent and pH-sensitive. The ability to consume GSH increased with the increase of dose and acidity. At high dose (solution pH = 5.5), the consumption rate of GSH by DTNB reached about 100 %, indicated that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc has a strong consumption capacity of GSH.

The quantum yield of 1O2 is an important parameter to evaluate the effect of photosensitizer PDT. Therefore, we investigate the singlet oxygen yield of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. DPBF is a commonly used 1O2 trapping agent. After oxidation, the absorption peak of DPBF at 412 nm weakens until it disappears [40]. The absorbance of DBPF is negatively correlated with the concentration of 1O2. In this experiment, MB, a well-known photosensitizer with a quantum yield of 49 % for singlet oxygen, was employed as a reference. Fig. 3m shown the rapid decomposition of DPBF in MB solution. Fig. 4 n and Fig. 4o shown the degradation of DPBF in Fe3O4-CDs@Fc and Fe3O4-CDs solution after laser irradiation, DPBF was rapidly degraded in these two solutions, but the degradation rate was slightly lower than that in MB solution. The degradation ability of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc was stronger than that of Fe3O4-CDs, which may be attributed to the surface-modified Fc. Its electron-absorbing ability improves light-absorbing effici-ency (Fig. 2a). Fig. 3p shown an excellent linear relationship between DPBF degradation rate (lnAt/A0, A is the DPBF absorbance) and time (R2 > 0.99). The singlet oxygen quantum yields of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc and Fe3O4-CDs are 33 % and 23 %, respectively, with the former being 10 % higher than the latter, indicating their PDT potential.

Prior to evaluating the cytotoxicity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc using MTT assay, we investigated the cellular uptake of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc by CT26 colon cancer cells. DAPI is a nuclear probe used for cell localization that displays blue fluorescence when stimulated by ultraviolet light. After Fe3O4-CDs@Fc is taken up by cells, it emits red light when stimulated with green light. The CT26 cells were observed under a fluorescence inverted microscope after co-incubation with Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (100 µg mL−1), as depicted in Fig. 4a. Intracellular red fluorescence showed time-dependent enhancement. After 3 h of co-incubation, the cells emitted strong bright red, indicated that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc has been successfully taken up by the CT26 cells. The results of MTT assay were shown in Fig. 4b, and there was a negative correlation between cell survival rate and dose. With the increase of dose, the survival rate of cells gradually decreased. At equal doses of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc and Fe3O4-CDs (calculated by Fe3O4 content), the former showed a stronger ability to inhibit the proliferation of tumor cells, which is attributed to the participation of Fc in Fenton reaction.

The survival rates of cells treated with Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (200 µg mL−1) were 10 % and 15 %, respectively, after individual irradiation with 808 nm and 660 nm lasers. The survival rate was 23 % in the absence of light, which showed a good synergistic effect of PTT/CDT and PDT/CDT. Fortunately, the combined irradiation of the two lasers (808 nm and 660 nm) for 10 min further reduced cell inhibition (5 %), demonstrated a highly effective CDT/PDT/PTT synergistic therapeutic effect. MTT assay was used to evaluate the toxicity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc on L929 cells (0, 50, 100, 200 and 200 µg mL−1 + Laser). The results were shown in Fig. S4. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc had no cytotoxicity on normal cells. Demonstrated therapeutic selectivity and biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Double staining of live and dead cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc and Fe3O4-CDs against cancer cells was evaluated used the live-dead cell double staining Kit (Calcein-AM/PI) [41]. Dead cells were stained red and living cells were stained green under a fluorescence microscope. A control group (saline), Fe3O4-CDs group, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc group, and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc + Laser group were established. The light group was exposed to 808 nm and 660 nm lasers for 10 min. The drug concentration for all groups was maintained at 200 µg mL−1, the results were shown in Fig. 4c. In the absence of light, both Fe3O4-CDs@Fc and Fe3O4-CDs shown remarkable anti-cancer ability due to their excellent Fenton reaction. However, the former was stronger because the Fc is involved in the reaction. Under the combined irradiation of 660 nm and 808 nm light, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc showed excellent inhibition of cancer cell growth and demonstrated a powerful CDT/PTT/PDT synergistic anticancer mechanism.

DCFH-DA is a non-fluorescent substance with the ability to freely cross the cell membrane. After entering the cell, DCFH is hydrolyzed by lactase to form DCFH. DCFH cannot cross the cell membrane, so that it remains in the cell [42]. Intracellular reactive oxygen species can oxidize non-fluorescent DCFH into fluorescent DCF. The green fluorescence (525 nm) was emitted upon excitation with a 480 nm laser, and the level of reactive oxygen species (ROS) was assessed by monitoring the change of intracellular green fluorescence using a fluorescence microscope. The control group (normal saline group) and the experimental groups [50, 100, 200 and 200 µg mL−1 + Laser (808 nm, 660 nm, 50 mW cm−2)] were established. As depicted in Fig. 4d, both Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc exhibited ROS generation in a concentration-dependent manner. In addition, intracellular ROS production was enhanced under 808 nm and 660 nm laser irradiation, demonstrated the synergistic potential of PDT/PTT/CDT. It is worth noting that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc shown better ROS generation ability than Fe3O4-CDs under the same conditions.

The level of intracellular GSH was determined by glutathione detection kit. CT26 cells were incubated with different concentrations of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc (0, 50, 100 and 200 µg mL−1), while a blank control group and a light group were set up for comparison. As shown in Fig. 5 a, intracellular relative glutathione content gradually decreases with the increase of drug concentration. When the dose reached 200 µg mL−1, the GSH content dropped to 40 %. Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc showed significant consumption of GSH. However, the latter shown superior performance. Under 808 nm laser irradiation, the GSH content decreased to 12 %, indicating that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc consumed 88 % of the cell GSH under photothermal action. These findings highlight the ability of photothermal assistance to enhance GSH depletion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The levels of intracellular oxygen and the expression of HIF-1α
ce:section.ce:para: RDPP probe was used to measure intracellular oxygen concentration, and its red fluorescence was negatively correlated with the intracellular oxygen concentration. Subsequently, Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc were introduced to anaerobically treated CT26 cells for comparative analysis of fluorescence intensity. Normal oxygen control and different concentration groups [0, 50, 100, 200 and 200 µg mL−1 + Laser (808 nm, power density 50 mW cm−2)] were established, and the results were shown in Fig. 5b. Both Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc exhibited a concentration-dependent increase intracellular oxygen concentration. This observation highlights the powerful self-supplying oxygen capacity of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc due to its excellent Fenton reaction ability. After 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min, the fluorescence of the cells was almost completely destroyed, showing strong oxygen supply capacity and photothermal synergistic oxygenation ability. The intracellular oxygen concentration and HIF-1 α were negatively correlated; therefore, the intracellular HIF-1α level was examined by immunofluorescence staining [43]. The green fluorescence represents HIF-1α expression, and the fluorescence intensity is positively correlated with the concentration level of HIF-1α. Fig. 5c shown that with the increase of the dose of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc the expression of HIF-1α was down-regulated, which was opposite to the trend of the increase of intracellular oxygen level, especially after irradiation with 808 nm laser, HIF-1α expression was almost completely suppressed, shown a strong photothermal-assisted effect to enhance oxygenation.

Tumor cell death induced immunogenic cell death, and high extracellular expression of calreticulin (CRT), adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and high mobility group protein (HMGB-1) were characteristic markers of ICD. CRT, ATP and HMGB-1 collectively constitute damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), which are crucial features of ICD [44–46]. During ICD in tumor cells, endoplasmic reticulum stress triggers the rapid translocation of significant amounts of CRT from the endoplasmic reticulum to the cell membrane surface (ecto-CRT), generating an “eat me” signal. ATP molecule acts as a chemokine to trigger the “eat me” signal and induce tumor cells to autophagy, resulting in the transformation of the tumor microenvironment from an anti-inflammatory state to a pro-inflammatory state. Subsequently, under the action of HMGB-1, the antigen is presented to T cells, resulting in the production and activation of effector T cells. These activated effector T cells then migrate and penetrate the tumor region, where they specifically recognize and eliminate tumor cells. CD8 molecules are markers of effector T cells. Interleukin-2(IL-2), as a differentiation factor of T cells, stimulates the function of other immune cells and plays a key role in coordinating the regulation of cellular immunity and humoral immunity. The high expression of IL-2 is an important index to activate immune activity [47]. Expression of ARG-1 is an indicator of the presence of immunosuppressive M2 tumor cell-associated macrophages, whose function is to protect tumor cells. Decreased ARG-1 expression marks a shift in the tumor microenvironment from immunosuppressive to antitumor [48]. Based on the above analysis, this study investigated the expression of ICD markers CRT and ATP in tumor cells and tissues. Subsequently, the expression of IL-2, CD8 and ARG-1 in mouse tumor tissues was detected. Fig. 6 a illustrates the results obtained from co-incubation of CT26 cells with Fe3O4-CDs@Fc for 4 h under a laser confocal microscope. The blue colour represents the nucleus stained with DIPA dye, while the green fluorescence indicates the fluorescence emitted by the CRT-antibody conjugate under blue light excitation. The green fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with the expression level of ecto-CRT. As shown in the figure, the green fluorescence on the cell surface was enhanced in a concentration-dependent manner, suggesting that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc induced exposion of CRT to the membrane surface. In addition, 660 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation further increased exposion levels of CRT, suggested that the CDT, PDT, and PTT effects of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc lead to ICD-associated CRT expression outside the cell. The synergistic effect of CDT/PDT/PTT is significant. ATP detection results showed that absorbance of CT26 cells was positively correlated with ATP expression level (Fig. 6b). ATP expression increased with the increase of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc dose, and ATP expression was further enhanced by 660 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation. This suggested that damage caused by CDT, PDT, and PTT leads to an increase in ICD and a similar trend of ATP change to that of ecto-CRT. The experiments of intratumoral tissue immunity in Balb/c mice was carried out using mouse double tumor model. The treatment plan follows Fig. 7 d. Immunofluorescence assay kit was used to analyze the immune response of primary tumor and distal tumor. These analyses revealed a dose-dependent up-regulation of CRT expression in tumor tissue (as shown by increased green fluorescence intensity). In addition, this upregulation was further enhanced after laser irradiation, as shown in Fig. 6c. It can be inferred that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc induced ICD, and laser radiation further enhanced ICD, and CDT/PDT/PTT synergistically enhances ICD. Fig. 6c showed that ARG-1 expression decreased significantly, indicated that the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (M2) was transformed into anti-tumor type (M1) type (green fluorescence was positively correlated with ARG-1 expression), and the expression of IL-2 was up-regulated (red fluorescence was positively correlated with IL-2 expression). The number of CD8+ T cells increased significantly with increasing dose (red fluorescence was positively correlated with the number of CD8+ T cells). In conclusion, it can be concluded that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc can induced ICD both in vitro and in vivo, leaded to the transformation of tumor microenvironment from immunosuppressive state to antitumor state. This results in activation of functional cells and systemic immune activity. When combined with the immune checkpoint inhibitor αPD-L1, the immune activity was further enhanced. It is worth noting that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc induced increased oxygen levels in tumor cells and tissues, decreased the expression of HIF-1α, and significantly increased the expression of immunoreactivity related proteins such as CRT, ATP, HMGB-1, IL-2, and CD8. Additionally, there was a notable decrease in the expression of the tumor immunosuppressant factor AGR-1. These findings suggest that the oxygen-enhancing effect of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc suppressed hypoxia-inducible factors which was positively correlating with immune activation protein activation to promote immune responses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in vivo distribution of Fe3O4-CDs@Fc
ce:section.ce:para: The CT26 cells were inoculated into the right posterior gluteal region of female Balb/c mice, aged seven weeks and weighing approximately 20 g. Once the tumor volume reached around 100 mm3, the Fe3O4-CDs@Fc drug was administered intravenously via the tail vein at a dosage of 100 µL (100 µg mL−1). Subsequently, magnets were used to expose the tumor site to a magnetic field for in vivo imaging and observation of drug distribution. Additionally, a thermal imager was employed to monitor changes in temperature at the tumor site after illumination. The results depicted in Fig. 7a demonstrates that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc exhibited a higher accumulation at the tumor site compared to other sites after 2 h of administration. This indicates the preferential enrichment of superparamagnetic Fe3O4-CDs@Fc at the tumor site under the influence of an external magnetic field. The enrichment reached its peak after 4 h, followed by gradual metabolism. At 16 h, there were still substantial residues at both the tumor site and other sites, while only a small amount remained in the body after 24 h. These results indicated that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc accumulated rapidly due to an external magnetic field. The thermal imaging images of mice are shown in Fig. 7d. There was no significant difference in temperature between tumor sites and other sites in the normal saline group. However, in the Fe3O4-CDs@Fc group, the temperature at the tumor site was significantly higher than at the other sites. This observation suggested that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc exhibited superior drug enrichment at the tumor site along with a highly efficient photo-thermal conversion effect, demonstrated excellent superparamagnetic targeting property. The temperature at the tumor site reached its peak after 4 h. However, only a weak photothermal effect was observed after 24 h, suggesting the presence of residual Fe3O4-CDs@Fc in small quantities. The results obtained from photothermal imaging were consistent with those from in vivo fluorescence imaging. Fig. 7b and Fig. 7c depict fluorescence imaging maps and drug distribution histograms of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumor taken at 4 h post-administration of the drug. These images clearly demonstrate that solid tumors exhibit the highest fluorescence intensity and drug concentration.

The in vivo antitumor treatment regimen was illustrated in Fig. 7e, wherein CT26 cells were inoculated bilaterally into the hindquarters of 7-week-old Balb/c mice. Tumors on the right side were designated as primary tumors (subjected to drug treatment), while tumors on the left side were defined as metastases (distal tumors with no treatment). When the tumor reached a volume of approximately 100 mm3, the mice were randomly allocated into six groups: control group (saline), αPD-L1 group, Fe3O4-CDs group, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc group, Fe3O4-CDs@Fc + Later group, and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc + αPD-L1 + Laser group. The drugs were administered via tail vein injection at a dose of 6 mg kg−1 on days 1 and 7. At 4 h after administration, 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min and 660 nm laser irradiation for 15 min, respectively, with optical power density 50 mW cm−2. The αPD-L1 was administered intraperitoneally at a dosage of 2.5 mg kg−1 per mouse following illumination. Daily recordings were made for body weights and tumor volumes, while mental status and the condition of fur were observed in the mice. On the 14th day, the mice were euthanized and vital organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, as well as tumor tissues were excised and weighed. Tissue organs and tumor sections underwent H&E staining for evaluation purposes. Immunofluorescence detection kit was used to assess immunosignature markers levels and expression of hypoxia marker HIF-1α in tumor tissues (Fig. 6c). Fig. 7f illustrated primary tumor entities (upper panel) along with distal tumor (lower panel), For the primary tumor, the control group grew without restriction, indicated that the tumor grew freely in the mice without drug treatment, and the tumor growth state of the αPD-L1 treatment group was similar to that of the control group, indicated that there was no inhibitory effect on tumor growth under the dose conditions in this study. In the other treatment groups, tumor growth was significantly inhibited in the order of III, IV, V, IV, demonstrated a highly efficient multi-mode synergistic treatment effect. For the distal tumor, the growth trend of the tumor was consistent with that of the primary tumor, indicated that Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc activated the immune activity of the system, and the combined immune checkpoint blocker (αPD-L1) effectively inhibited the growth of the distal tumor. Fig. 7g presented volume change curve of proximal tumors during treatment period, Fig. 7h shown volume change curve of the proximal tumor during treatment period again, Fig. 7j exhibited mass measurements of both proximal and distal tumor at end of treatment period. It can be seen that the volume and weight of solid tumors showed the same trend, the in-situ tumor were efficiently ablated, and the growth trend of untreated distal tumor was effectively inhibited. Fig. 7i shown that the weight of the mice remained largely unchanged throughout the treatment period, indicated that the treatment was mild and the drug was biocompatible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The histological examination of tumor and various organs
ce:section.ce:para: The H&E staining of major organs and tumor tissue sections after 14 days of treatment in the dual-tumor model mice is presented in Fig. 8 . The heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys exhibit clear striations and smooth surfaces without any visible damage to the naked eye, indicating the exceptional biocompatibility of Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc. Both primary and distal tumors showed clear signs of injury, including tissue shedding, stomata, severe cell atrophy, and tumor cell death. In distal tumors, cell death observed that Fe3O4-CDs and Fe3O4-CDs@Fc induced ICD, leaded to systemic immune activation, and that treatment in combination with immune checkpoint blockers leaded to more pronounced damage in distal tumors, suggested greater immune reactivity.

Fe3O4-CDs was successfully prepared by one-pot method, and ferrocene was modified on the surface to obtain Fe3O4-CDs@Fc nanoparticles. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc catalyzed the Fenton reaction, improved the reaction pH condition to 7.4, and enhanced the Fenton reaction efficiency. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc efficiently catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2, increased the intracellular oxygen concentration of tumor tissue, and inhibited the expression of HIF-1α. Systemic immune activity was induced by powerful multimodal synergistic (CDT/PDT/PTT) therapy. In vitro and in vivo experiments showed that Fe3O4-CDs@Fc combined immune checkpoint blocker (αPD-L1) has good primary tumor clearance and remote tumor inhibition effect, and is expected to be a candidate drug to inhibit tumor metastasis and recurrence. Fe3O4-CDs@Fc has a good prospect in clinical anti-tumor immunotherapy.
